coredata.dc:title: Inorganic salt recrystallization strategy for achieving ultralong room temperature phosphorescence through structural confinement and aluminized reconstruction
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Liang, Li Ya
Chen, Bin Bin
Wang, Yue
Gao, Ya Ting
Chang, Shuai
Liu, Meng Li
Li, Da Wei
coredata.dc:description: Achieving highly efficient and stable room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) with ultralong lifetime is critical for the multi-purpose applications of phosphorescent materials. In this work, we propose an inorganic salt heating recrystallization strategy to simultaneously improve the lifetime, quantum efficiency, and stability of phosphorescent scandium/leucine microspheres (Sc/Leu-MSs). Inorganic salt-treated Sc/Leu-MSs are obtained by simply heating and drying inorganic salt solution containing Sc/Leu-MSs, which can achieve a maximum lifetime increase of 4.42-times from 208.37 ms (Sc/Leu-MSs) to 920.08 ms (Al2(SO4)3-treated Sc/Leu-MSs), accompanied by a RTP intensity increase up to 24.08-times. The enhancement mechanism of RTP efficiency is attributed to the stabilization of triplet excitons caused by inorganic salt coating that suppresses molecular motion and isolates oxygen on the one hand, and the efficient intersystem crossing promoted by aluminized reconstruction-caused duplex heavy atom effects on the other hand. This study provides new design principle and a facile strategy to construct RTP materials with ultralong lifetime, high phosphorescent quantum efficiency, and high stability for promising applications such as anti-counterfeiting and light emitting diodes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Rare earth microsphere
Room temperature phosphorescence
Inorganic salt heating recrystallization
Structural confinement
Duplex heavy atom effects
Aluminized reconstruction

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Formation and characterization of scandium/leucine microspheres
Inorganic salt recrystallization strategies of scandium/leucine microspheres

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Morphology and composition of Sc/Leu-MSs. (a, b) SEM images of Sc/Leu-MSs. (c) SEM image of Sc/Leu-MSs section. (d) Element mapping of Sc/Leu-MSs. (e) FT-IR spectra of Leu ligand, Sc/Leu complex, and Sc/Leu-MSs. (f) High-resolution O1s XPS spectrum, (g) high-resolution Sc2p XPS spectrum, and (h) XRD spectrum of Sc/Leu-MSs.

Optical properties of Sc/Leu-MSs. (a) Absorption spectrum, (b) laser confocal imaging, (c) 3D phos. spectrum, (d) phos. emission spectra at different excitations, (e) phos. excitation spectra, (f) PL QYs at different excitations, and (g) phos. lifetimes at different excitations and emissions of Sc/Leu-MSs. (h) The possible migration pathways of excited-state electrons in Sc/Leu-MSs.
Optical properties of Leu and Sc/Leu solid. (a) Phos. spectrum and (b) lifetime of Leu solid. Excitation: 280 nm. (c) Phos. spectrum and (d) lifetime of Sc/Leu solid. Excitation: 330 nm.

Optical properties of inorganic salt@Sc/Leu-MSs. (a) Photographs of (i) Sc/Leu-MSs, (ii) K2SO4@Sc/Leu-MSs, (iii) CaSO4@Sc/Leu-MSs, (iv) Na2SO4@Sc/Leu-MSs, (v) MgSO4@Sc/Leu-MSs, (vi) Al2(SO4)3@Sc/Leu-MSs, and (vii) ZnSO4@Sc/Leu-MSs powder taken before and after removing 302 nm UV light. (b) 3D phos. spectra and (c) maximum normalized phos. emission spectra of six inorganic salt@Sc/Leu-MSs. (d) Phos. lifetimes of six inorganic salt@Sc/Leu-MSs at different excitations. (e) Mass of six inorganic salt@Sc/Leu-MSs obtained after one heating recrystallization treatment.
Morphology and composition of inorganic salt@Sc/Leu-MSs. (a) SEM images and (b) element mapping of inorganic salt@Sc/Leu-MSs.

Structural characterizations of Sc/Leu-MSs, K2SO4@Sc/Leu-MSs and Al2(SO4)3@Sc/Leu-MSs. High-resolution Sc2p spectra of (a) K2SO4@Sc/Leu-MSs and (b) Al2(SO4)3@Sc/Leu-MSs. High-resolution O1s spectra of (c) K2SO4@Sc/Leu-MSs and (d) Al2(SO4)3@Sc/Leu-MSs. (e) Al2p spectrum of Al2(SO4)3@Sc/Leu-MSs. (f) FT-IR spectra of K2SO4@Sc/Leu-MSs and Al2(SO4)3@Sc/Leu-MSs. (g and h) XRD spectra of Sc/Leu-MSs, K2SO4@Sc/Leu-MSs and Al2(SO4)3@Sc/Leu-MSs. (i) 3D phos. spectrum of Al/Leu-PMs.
The effect of the proposed recrystallization strategy on structure and RTP efficiency of the Sc/Leu-MSs.

Applications of Sc/Leu-MSs and Al2(SO4)3@Sc/Leu-MSs. (a) Digital photographs of information encryption (rose and tree patterns) made from Sc/Leu-MSs. Two fluorescent materials without phos. emissions (europium and terbium micro-networks) are used to assist in the design of tree pattern. Excitation: 365 nm. (b) Fluorescent and phos. images of picture and Chinese characters written in Sc/Leu-MSs suspension. Excitation: 365 nm. (c) The chromaticity coordinates of DUV-LEDs under a drive current of 60 mA in CIE 1931 colour spaces. (d) The emission spectra of DUV-LEDs device under different drive currents. Excitation: 285 nm (e) Photographs of the as-fabricated DUV-LEDs lighted ones.
Schematic illustration of the enhancement mechanism of RTP efficiency of Sc/Leu-MSs by an inorganic salt heating recrystallization strategy.

head.ce:title: Inorganic salt recrystallization strategy for achieving ultralong room temperature phosphorescence through structural confinement and aluminized reconstruction

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shanghai Key Laboratory of Functional Materials Chemistry

East China University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An inorganic salt heating recrystallization strategy is proposed to simultaneously improve the phosphorescent lifetime, quantum efficiency and stability of phosphorescent scandium/leucine microspheres (Sc/Leu-MSs), which is attributed to the stabilization of triplet excitons caused by inorganic salt coating and efficient intersystem crossing induced by duplex heavy atom effects.

Achieving highly efficient and stable room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) with ultralong lifetime is critical for the multi-purpose applications of phosphorescent materials. In this work, we propose an inorganic salt heating recrystallization strategy to simultaneously improve the lifetime, quantum efficiency, and stability of phosphorescent scandium/leucine microspheres (Sc/Leu-MSs). Inorganic salt-treated Sc/Leu-MSs are obtained by simply heating and drying inorganic salt solution containing Sc/Leu-MSs, which can achieve a maximum lifetime increase of 4.42-times from 208.37 ms (Sc/Leu-MSs) to 920.08 ms (Al2(SO4)3-treated Sc/Leu-MSs), accompanied by a RTP intensity increase up to 24.08-times. The enhancement mechanism of RTP efficiency is attributed to the stabilization of triplet excitons caused by inorganic salt coating that suppresses molecular motion and isolates oxygen on the one hand, and the efficient intersystem crossing promoted by aluminized reconstruction-caused duplex heavy atom effects on the other hand. This study provides new design principle and a facile strategy to construct RTP materials with ultralong lifetime, high phosphorescent quantum efficiency, and high stability for promising applications such as anti-counterfeiting and light emitting diodes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials with long-lived excitons have attracted wide attention because of their unique advantages in low background-interference, enhanced signal-to-noise ratio, and long-lived emission,[1–3] which exhibit good application prospects in sensing[4], information encryption[5], cellular imaging[6], and light-emitting diodes (LEDs)[7]. From the application perspective, construction of stable RTP materials with highly phosphorescent quantum yield (QYs) and long lifetime above millisecond level is essential, but it is a significant challenge because triplet exciton is very sensitive to many factors such as humidity, environmental oxygen, and rigid structure.[8,9] Except for improving intersystem crossing (ISC) efficiency, the design concept of efficient RTP materials is primarily focused on suppressing the non-radiative and oxygen quenching deactivation of triplet excitons.

Molecular design can improve quantum efficiency and prolong lifetime of RTP materials by regulating their structure,[10] such as the introduction of the carbazolyl group[11], heavy halogen[12], or aromatic aldehyde group[13]. To achieve RTP materials with excellent efficiency, it is also useful to utilize the confinement structure strategy, namely by creating hybrid systems of RTP materials in porous organic or inorganic matrices,[14] such as metal–organic frameworks[15], zeolites[16], layered double hydroxides[17], and Mn-containing open-framework[18], and so forth. By suppressing molecular motion and minimizing oxygen-mediated non-radiative quenching, the confinement effect can improve the luminescent efficiency and lifetime of phosphorescent materials on the basis of their original optical parameters.[19].

However, current confinement strategies are still influenced by a variety of factors, resulting in an unsatisfactory lifetime and quantum efficiency. For starters, limited by the pore size of the matrices, large-sized phosphorescent materials can only be embedded in the interrupted nano-space or attached to the external surface of the matrices.[14] Second, complex nanoscale force and solvent have a significant impact on the loading efficiency of phosphorescent materials in porous matrices.[16] Finally, solvent or oxygen molecules can still enter the porous structure of the matrices and quench the triplet excitons of phosphorescent materials. Because of these unavoidable factors, current confinement strategies are not enough to achieve the desired RTP performance.[14] Furthermore, due to the complex synthetic operations and expensive equipment, those commonly used matrices are not widely available. As a result, the development of new design strategies to effectively stabilize the triplet excitons of phosphorescent materials in order to achieve long-lived and efficient RTP is highly desirable but challenging.

In this study, we synthesize micron-sized scandium/leucine microspheres (Sc/Leu-MSs) by the hydrothermal polymerization of the complex consisting of Sc3+ ions and Leu (Sc/Leu complex). At a single excitation wavelength, the formed Sc/Leu-MSs emit excitation-independent cyan (490 nm) and deep red (695 nm) phosphorescent emissions with a dominant lifetime of 208.37 ms. Importantly, an inorganic salt heating recrystallization strategy is developed for achieving the ultralong lifetime and high quantum efficiency of Sc/Leu-MSs by employing a variety of inorganic salt solution. As shown in Scheme 1 , owing to the suppression of molecular motion and acting as a protective layer that protects triplet excitons from oxygen-mediated quenching deactivation, the resulting K2SO4-treated Sc/Leu-MSs (K2SO4@Sc/Leu-MSs) can achieve a lifetime increase of 2.78-times and a RTP intensity increase of 4.19-times. Whereas, Al2(SO4)3-treated Sc/Leu-MSs (Al2(SO4)3@Sc/Leu-MSs) have a lifetime increase of up to 4.42-times and a phosphorescent intensity increase of up to 24.08-times. The significantly improved RTP efficiency is attributed to efficient ISC promoted by aluminized reconstruction-caused duplex heavy atom effects, in addition to the confinement environment provided by Al2(SO4)3. The formed Al2(SO4)3@Sc/Leu-MSs with exceptional optical properties provide potential applications in advanced phosphorescent anti-counterfeiting and deep ultraviolet (UV)-excited LEDs (DUV-LEDs). This research not only deepens our understanding of the RTP mechanism, but also provides a powerful and facile strategy for large-scale and low-cost synthesis of phosphorescent materials with ultralong lifetime.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Formation and characterization of scandium/leucine microspheres
ce:section.ce:para:
The phosphorescent Sc/Leu-MSs can be prepared by a facile one-pot hydrothermal polymerization of Sc3 + ions and Leu ligands. As shown in Fig. 1 a, the proposed Sc/Leu-MSs have a quasi-microsphere structure with an average diameter of approximately 5 μm ( Figure S1 , Supporting Information ). Sc/Leu-MSs have a rough surface texture, as evidenced by a scanning electron microscopy (SEM) image of a typical microsphere (Fig. 1b). Furthermore, the SEM image of the Sc/Leu-MSs section shows a distinct hollow structure (Fig. 1c). SEM images from different batches of Sc/Leu-MSs show that they all have the same microsphere structure with a rough surface texture ( Figure S2 , Supporting Information ), indicating that the developed hydrothermal method has a good repeatability. The overall elemental composition of Sc/Leu-MSs, which are primarily composed of C, O, and Sc elements, is revealed by energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) ( Figure S3 , Supporting Information
). The uniform element distribution in the Sc/Leu-MSs is shown by element mapping ( Fig. 1d), indicating that the Sc/Leu-MSs are formed homogeneously.

The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra ( Fig. 1e) reveal the mechanism of Sc/Leu-MSs formation. Leu molecules exhibit typical peaks at 2960, 1614, 1407, and 1131 cm−1, corresponding to C H stretching vibration, N H bending vibration, C O stretching vibration, and C N stretching vibration, respectively.[20] Following the formation of Sc/Leu complexes, two new strong bands appear at 1301 and 1653 cm−1, corresponding to the symmetric and asymmetric stretching of the carboxylate groups, implying the coordination of Sc3+ ions and carboxyl groups. Meanwhile, there is no shift in the N H bending vibration after the formation of Sc/Leu complexes and Sc/Leu-MSs, indicating that there is no coordination between Sc3+ ions and amino groups. As a result, Sc3+ ions form Sc/Leu complexes in aqueous solution by coordinating with the carboxyl groups of Leu molecules. It is noteworthy that the multi-site coordination ability of Sc3+ ions, which can further coordinate with the residual uncoordinated carboxyl groups at high temperature.[21,22] As a result, Sc/Leu-MSs can be formed by the aggregation of Sc/Leu complexes.

After forming Sc/Leu-MSs, the symmetric and asymmetric stretching of the carboxylate groups shifts to 1325 and 1573 cm−1, causing the vibration frequency difference between the symmetric and asymmetric stretching to decrease from 352 cm−1 (Sc/Leu complexes) to 248 cm−1 (Sc/Leu-MSs). This decrease suggests that the carboxyl groups of Leu molecules effectively bond to Sc3+ ions.[23] The characteristic peak at 530.4 eV in the high-resolution O1s X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) spectrum ( Fig. 1f) confirms the existence of the Sc − O bond.[24] Meanwhile, due to the obvious split spin–orbit components (ΔE = 4.6 eV), the Sc2p XPS spectrum reveals the presence of Sc in the form of Sc3+ ions ( Fig. 1g).[25] These results further reveal the coordination of Sc3+ ions and carboxyl groups by Sc − O bond. Sc/Leu-MSs have poor crystallinity, according to powder X-ray diffractometer (XRD) spectrum, due to their broad diffraction band ( Fig. 1h). These findings suggest that the formation of Sc/Leu-MSs begins with Sc3+ ions and Leu coordination by Sc − O bond and the subsequent high temperature-promoted disordered aggregation of Sc/Leu complex due to the multi-site coordination ability of Sc3+ ions.

Thermogravimetric (TG) and differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) analysis reveal the thermal stability of Sc/Leu-MSs, which show that Sc/Leu-MSs have a weight loss of about 10% at temperature below 370 °C, with an endothermic reaction peak at 458 °C on the DSC curve (
Figure S4
,
Supporting Information
). The loss of physisorbed and coordinated water molecules causes the slow weight loss below 370 °C.[26,27] Sc/Leu-MSs can partially decompose at temperatures above 370 °C, resulting in rapid and continuous weight loss. This should be attributed to the abundant carboxylate group of Sc/Leu-MSs because the decarboxylation can start at a high temperature of about 300 °C without catalyst.[28] The higher the temperature, the easier the decarboxylation reaction is to proceed. When the temperature exceeds about 500 °C, the decarboxylation is basically completed, thus slowing down the weight loss until the weight loss stops. Whereas, Sc/Leu-MSs retain 49% by weight even at a high temperature of 800 °C, indicating their good thermal stability.

The Sc/Leu-MSs have strong UV absorption with two shoulder peaks at 250 nm and 325 nm ( Fig. 2 a) and an optical band gap energy of about 3.3 eV (
Figure S5
,
Supporting Information
). The Sc/Leu-MSs have an excitation-independent fluorescence (FL) with a maximum emission at 390 nm when excited at 330 nm, according to the three-dimensional (3D) fluorescent spectrum (
Figure S6
,
Supporting Information
). Meanwhile, laser confocal imaging ( Fig. 2b) clearly shows the hollow structure of the Sc/Leu-MSs, indicating that Sc/Leu-MSs can act as the single particle imaging probe. At room temperature, the prepared Sc/Leu-MSs can simultaneously emit excitation-independent cyan (490 nm) and deep red (695 nm) phosphorescent emissions ( Fig. 2c and d). That is to say, the phosphorescent emission peaks of the Sc/Leu-MSs remain at about 490 and 695 nm when the excitation wavelength increases from 250 nm to 370 nm. The normalized RTP emission spectra at different excitations show a good overlap (
Figure S7
,
Supporting Information
), which further confirms the excitation-independent emission feature of Sc/Leu-MSs. According to the excitation spectrum ( Fig. 2e), the cyan RTP can be excited at a wide range of UV wavelengths from 250 to 450 nm, whereas the deep red RTP can only be excited at high-energy UV wavelengths less than 320 nm.

As shown in Fig. 2f, the photoluminescence (PL) QY of the cyan luminescent component is about 21.99%, which is about 15.60 times higher than that of the deep red luminescent component (1.41%). Likewires, the RTP lifetime of cyan luminescent component is about 208.37 ms, which is about 21.03-times greater than that of deep red luminescent component (9.91 ms) ( Fig. 2g). These findings strongly suggest that cyan RTP is the dominant luminescence of the Sc/Leu-MSs. The transition process of excited electrons is shown in Fig. 2h, the excited-state electrons under high-energy UV light excitation (270 nm) can return to the ground state via dual transition channels corresponding to cyan RTP and deep red RTP, whereas excited-state electrons under low-energy UV light excitation (350 nm) can return to the ground state via a single transition channel corresponding to cyan RTP.

The luminescent mechanism of Sc/Leu-MSs is further explored. As shown in Fig. 3 a and b, Leu solid powders emit weak cyan phos. emission at about 490 nm when excited by 280 nm UV light at room temperature, showing a short lifetime of 1.88 ms. The luminescence of nonaromatic Leu is thought to be caused by the clustering of amino and carbonyl groups, followed by electron cloud overlap and simultaneous conformation rigidification.[29] The lifetime of the Sc/Leu solid, which is formed by heating and drying a mixed solution of Sc3+ ions and Leu ligands at 90 °C, increases to 91.53 ms ( Fig. 3c and d) due to the improvement of RTP efficiency. Such alterations should be caused by the Sc3+ ions, which can act as heavy atoms to effectively promote ISC efficiency and achieve efficient phosphorescent emission. Compared with Sc/Leu solid, there is a stronger coordination effect between Sc3+ ions and Leu ligands in Sc/Leu-MSs because of the high hydrothermal polymerization temperature. This greatly improves the ISC of excited electrons due to the heavy atom effect caused by Sc3+ ions, resulting in bright RTP emission of the Sc/Leu-MSs. Meanwhile, the dual phos. emission properties of Sc/Leu-MSs may be attributed to the presence of multiple emissive species.[30] During the process of hydrothermal polymerization, the coordination between Leu molecules and Sc3+ ions may form different aggregation states of Sc/Leu complexes, which causes the dual phos. emission properties of the Sc/Leu-MSs. The enlargement of aggregation state or cluster can result in a red-shifted phos. emission.[31].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Inorganic salt recrystallization strategies of scandium/leucine microspheres
ce:section.ce:para:
Common porous matrices are difficult to use for confining large-sized Sc/Leu-MSs. Inorganic salt heating recrystallization strategy is found to be able to effectively achieve the structural confinement of micron-sized Sc/Leu-MSs. As shown in Fig. 4 a, after being treated with six different inorganic salts (K2SO4, CaSO4, Na2SO4, MgSO4, Al2(SO4)3, ZnSO4), the phos. lifetimes and intensities of Sc/Leu-MSs, particularly those treated with Al2(SO4)3, have significantly increased. The amount of Al2(SO4)3 has a significant impact on the enhancement of the RTP lifetime of Sc/Leu-MSs (
Figure S8
,
Supporting Information
), and the prepared Al2(SO4)3@Sc/Leu-MSs exhibit the best phos. efficiency when using 1.0 M Al2(SO4)3 solution (4 mL) for recrystallization. Phos. spectra ( Fig. 4b and c) display that different inorganic salt treatments have different effects on the phos. properties of Sc/Leu-MSs. After treatment with K2SO4, CaSO4, Na2SO4, and MgSO4, the Sc/Leu-MSs retain their original dual phos. emission characteristics, indicating that these four inorganic salts have no effect on the structure of Sc/Leu-MSs. However, treatment with Al2(SO4)3 and ZnSO4 converts the dual phos. emission (490 nm and 695 nm) of Sc/Leu-MSs to a single phos. emission (450 nm), indicating structural transformation of Sc/Leu-MSs. Meanwhile, inorganic salt treatment also enhances the phos. emission intensity of the Sc/Leu-MSs, especially those treated with Al2(SO4)3, with a maximum enhancement factor of about 24.08 times (
Figure S9
,
Supporting Information
). These results indicate that inorganic salt treatment can effectively enhance the phos. efficiency of Sc/Leu-MSs, including the lifetime and intensity of phos. emission.

At least three significant advantages are demonstrated by the proposed recrystallization strategy. First, the inorganic salt treatment effectively extends the RTP lifetime of the Sc/Leu-MSs ( Fig. 4d and
Table S1
,
Supporting Information
), which can be increased by 4.42-times to 920.08 ms at most, achieving the ultralong RTP lifetime. Second, inorganic salt treatment can cause a maximum phos. intensity increase of 24.08 times. Third, the mass of Sc/Leu-MSs can significantly increase after inorganic salt treatment, and can be at least 27.16-times higher than the initial mass of Sc/Leu-MSs ( Fig. 4e), providing a powerful strategy for large-scale and low-cost synthesis of RTP materials with ultralong lifetime. Moreover, the proposed recrystallization strategy has also a good repeatability becasue the Al2(SO4)3@Sc/Leu-MSs from different batches have similar RTP lifetimes (
Figure S10
,
Supporting Information
).

To gain an in-depth insight into the mechanism of inorganic salt-induced RTP efficiency enhancement, different inorganic salts on the morphology of Sc/Leu-MSs are carried out clearly based on the SEM images ( Fig. 5 a). Sc/Leu-MSs retain their microsphere structure after recrystallization with K2SO4, CaSO4, Na2SO4, or MgSO4, but their rough surface texture becomes smooth, indicating that the Sc/Leu-MSs have an inorganic salt coating layer on their surface. This result indicates that the inorganic salt recrystallization strategy can effectively achieve uniform coating of Sc/Leu-MSs. When treated with Al2(SO4)3 or ZnSO4, the Sc/Leu-MSs will transform into a non-microsphere structure, indicating that these two inorganic salts can competitively coordinate with Leu to achieve structural reconstruction. Although these two new structures are not microsphere anymore, they have the best performance. Element mapping clearly shows the various interactions between inorganic salts and Sc/Leu-MSs ( Fig. 5b). Sc/Leu-MSs and sulfate are represented by Sc and S/metal elements in the mapping images, respectively. According to the findings, K2SO4, CaSO4, Na2SO4, and MgSO4 can form a protective layer on the surface of Sc/Leu-MSs without affecting their microphere structure. While Al2(SO4)3 and ZnSO4 can effectively destroy the microsphere structure of Sc/Leu-MSs, and they can form a new structure with uniform Sc, S, and Al atom distribution. K2SO4@Sc/Leu-MSs and Al2(SO4)3@Sc/Leu-MSs are two typical cases studied further for structural changes.

The change in surface element composition and chemical structure of Sc/Leu-MSs is analyzed with emphasis by the XPS technique. Sc/Leu-MSs are primarily composed of C (285.2 eV), O (532.0 eV), and Sc (402.8 eV) atoms, with a Sc atomic percentage of 10.49 at% (
Figure S11
,
Supporting Information
). The percentage of Sc atoms on the surface decreases significantly to about 1 at% after the formation of K2SO4@Sc/Leu-MSs and Al2(SO4)3@Sc/Leu-MSs. The peak position of Sc2p does not obviously shift after the formation of K2SO4@Sc/Leu-MSs compared with Sc/Leu-MSs ( Fig. 6 a), but its strength is significantly reduced because the K2SO4 coating layer reduces surface Sc content. However, after forming Al2(SO4)3@Sc/Leu-MSs, the peak position of Sc2p clearly shifts toward higher energy levels ( Fig. 6b), indicating changes in the coordination environment of Sc3+ ions. Meanwhile, a peak near 401.9 eV is attributed to the Al − N bond ( Fig. 6b),[32] indicating the coordination of Al3+ ions with amino groups. It should be noted that Sc3+ ions still have a considerable proportion after the formation of Al2(SO4)3@Sc/Leu-MSs and ZnSO4@Sc/Leu-MSs.

High-resolution O1s XPS spectra reveal additional structural changes in Sc/Leu-MSs. After the formation of K2SO4@Sc/Leu-MSs, the Sc − O bond in Sc/Leu-MSs remains ( Fig. 6c), but its percentage decreases significantly from 22.99% (Sc/Leu-MSs) to 4.25% (K2SO4@Sc/Leu-MSs) (
Table S2
,
Supporting Information
), implying the coating of K2SO4 on the surface of Sc/Leu-MSs. By contrast, for the Sc/Leu-MSs treated with Al2(SO4)3, the Sc-O bond disappears directly, and instead, a typical peak of the σ* transition of Al-O bond appears at 534.9 eV ( Fig. 6d).[33] Meanwhile, the Al2p spectrum reveals that Al exists as Al-O and Al-N bonds in Al2(SO4)3@Sc/Leu-MSs ( Fig. 6e).[34] Consequently, these results reveal that the Al3+ ions-induced reconstruction of Sc/Leu-MSs structure is caused by the stronger coordination between Al3+ ions and amino/carboxyl groups than the coordination of Sc3+ ions and carboxyl groups, thereby leading to a reshaping of the structure through the formation of Al-O and Al-N bonds.

Following that, FT-IR spectra are used to demonstrate the change of surface chemical groups of Sc/Leu-MSs. As shown in Fig. 6f, after K2SO4 treatment, the vibrational and rotational peaks of all chemical groups from the Sc/Leu-MSs almost disappear, except for the peak of SO4 2− groups (1114 cm− 1),[35] indicating that the coating effect of K2SO4 can greatly inhibit the movement of surface groups of the Sc/Leu-MSs, thereby improving phos. efficiency by suppressing the non-radiative and oxygen quenching deactivation of triplet excitons. Moreover, Al2(SO4)3@Sc/Leu-MSs show a broad absorption band at about 3100 cm−1 and a strong peak at about 1638 cm−1, which are assigned to the hydroxyl groups associated with physisorbed water bound by weak hydrogen bonds.[36] Importantly, the absorption peak of 922 cm−1 is attributed to the metal–oxygen bond such as Sc − O/Al − O bond.[37] However, the vibrational and rotational peaks such as C N and C O bonds from molecular ligand almost disappear as well, indicating that most Al2(SO4)3 is not involved in coordination, but serves as a filling medium to suppress molecular motion. XRD spectra further reflect the structural changes of the Sc/Leu-MSs. After K2SO4 treatment, the XRD spectrum of K2SO4@Sc/Leu-MSs is almost identical to that of K2SO4 ( Fig. 6g), indicating that there is no interaction between K2SO4 and Sc/Leu-MSs. However, XRD spectrum of the Al2(SO4)3@Sc/Leu-MSs differs from that of Al2(SO4)3, but the main peaks can correspond ( Fig. 6h). This finding strongly suggests that only a portion of Al2(SO4)3 participates in the competitive coordination of Sc/Leu-MSs by Al − O and Al − N bonds, and the remainder acting as a filling medium to suppresses molecular motion and prevent oxygen-mediated quenching of triplet excitons.

The Al/Leu phosphorescent materials (Al/Leu-PMs) are synthesized to gain a deeper insight into the role of Al3+ ions in the phos. emission of Al2(SO4)3@Sc/Leu-MSs. SEM image (
Figure S12
,
Supporting Information
) demonstrates that Al/Leu-PMs have a regular structure and emit strong phos. emission with a maximum emission at 450 nm when excited at 290 nm ( Fig. 6i). The 3D phos. emission of Al/Leu-PMs is nearly identical to that of Al2(SO4)3@Sc/Leu-MSs, indicating that the Al2(SO4)3-induced phos. emission change of Sc/Leu-MSs is due to the aluminized reconstruction effect. However, the lifetime (430.85 ms) of Al/Leu-PMs is only about half that of Al2(SO4)3@Sc/Leu-MSs (
Figure S13
,
Supporting Information
). This is because duplex heavy atom effects (Sc and Al) exist in Al2(SO4)3@Sc/Leu-MSs whereas Al/Leu-PMs do not.

These findings show that K2SO4 is only coated on the surface of Sc/Leu-MSs during recrystallization, with no effect on the structure and dual phos. emission properties of Sc/Leu-MSs. The K2SO4 coating layer effectively isolates oxygen and suppresses molecular motion, stabilizing the triplet excitons of Sc/Leu-MSs and resulting in a long lifetime RTP of K2SO4@Sc/Leu-MSs. On the contrary, Al3+ ions can competely coordinate with amino and carboxyl groups via Al − O and Al − N bonds, thereby disintegrating the microsphere structure of Sc/Leu-MSs. This aluminized reconstruction makes the composition of the phosphorescent material uniform, resulting in a dual-transition mode to a single-transition mode. As a comparison, except for Al2(SO4)3, pure inorganic salts (K2SO4, CaSO4, Na2SO4, MgSO4, and ZnSO4) have no emission under UV light irradiation (
Figure S14
a,
Supporting Information
). Al2(SO4)3 powder has a maximum phos. emission of about 435 nm when excited at 250 nm, with a RTP lifetime of 412.41 ms (
Figure S14
b and c,
Supporting Information
). However, the phos. emission of the Al2(SO4)3 powder is different from that of Al2(SO4)3@Sc/Leu-MSs. Meanwhile, it is found that the RTP lifetime of the Al2(SO4)3@Sc/Leu-MSs actually decreases when excessive Al2(SO4)3 is used. Most importantly, non-luminescent ZnSO4 also has a strong RTP enhancement efficiency on Sc/Leu-MSs. Therefore, although Al2(SO4)3 can emit RTP, the phos. emission of the Al2(SO4)3@Sc/Leu-MSs should mainly come from duplex heavy atom effects induced by Al3+ ions and Sc3+ ions. Considering that there is still uncoordinated Al2(SO4)3 in Al2(SO4)3@Sc/Leu-MSs, the phos. enhancement of Al2(SO4)3@Sc/Leu-MSs by Al2(SO4)3 cannot be completely ruled out.

The mechanism of RTP enhancement is analysed in detail. Leu solid shows a weak phos. emission and a short lifetime of 1.88 ms due to the clusteroluminescence. After introducing Sc3+ ions to form Sc/Leu-MSs, the RTP efficiency, including lifetime and intensity, has been significantly improved. This result reveals that the coordination of Sc3+ ions can greatly promote the ISC from singlet to triplet excited states by the promoted spin − orbit coupling.[38–40] After Al2(SO4)3 treatment, duplex heavy atom effects can further improve ISC efficiency due to the coexistence of Al3+ ions and Sc3+ ions in Al2(SO4)3@Sc/Leu-MSs, thus achieving the ultralong RTP. Meanwhile, Al2(SO4)3 crystals that are not involved in coordination act as filling media with phos. emission to suppress nonradiative relaxation processes due to the inhibition of molecular motion and prevent oxygen-mediated quenching of triplet excitons. Based on this, Al2(SO4)3@Sc/Leu-MSs exhibit the strongest phos. emission and ultralong RTP lifetime. The effect of the proposed recrystallization strategy on structure and RTP efficiency of the Sc/Leu-MSs is clearly shown in Fig. 7 .

Inspired by the good RTP properties, the Sc/Leu-MSs are employed as luminescent materials for phosphorescent anti-counterfeiting. Fig. 8 a shows the blue fluorescent “rose” pattern under 365 nm UV lamp radiation, and the strong green RTP pattern when the lamp is turned off. Furthermore, two fluorescent materials (red europium micro-networks and green terbium micro-networks)[26] are used in conjunction with Sc/Leu-MSs to create a “tree” pattern. The multicolor fluorescent “tree” pattern is visible under a 365 nm UV lamp. When the UV light is turned off, the main part of the “tree” pattern fades away, leaving only the green RTP pattern. Meanwhile, Sc/Leu-MSs can be used to create phosphorescent ink. Sc/Leu-MSs suspension can be used to handwrite on paper, displaying bright blue FL under 365 nm UV light and green RTP pattern when the UV light is turned off ( Fig. 8b).

Al2(SO4)3@Sc/Leu-MSs have a high potential for use in DUV-LEDs due to their excellent optical properties. DUV-LEDs are prepared by combining 285 nm UV chips to evaluate the device performance of the proposed Al2(SO4)3@Sc/Leu-MSs. In CIE 1931 color spaces, the chromaticity coordinate of DUV-LEDs with a color purity of 51.6 and a drive current of 60 mA, (0.2106, 0.2238) is marked ( Fig. 8c and
Table S3
,
Supporting Information
). Furthermore, as the drive current is increased from 20 mA to 120 mA, the emission intensities of DUV-LEDs gradually increase ( Fig. 8d). However, there are only minor variations in the values of chromaticity coordinates, color render index, and color purity for the DUV-LEDs devices when the drive current is changed (
Table S3
,
Supporting Information
). Fig. 8e shows photographs of the prepared DUV-LEDs as well as two lighted ones. These findings indicate that Al2(SO4)3@Sc/Leu-MSs have great potential for use in the development of a new type of DUV-LED.

In conclusion, this study has developed a facile inorganic salt recrystallization strategy for achieving ultralong RTP lifetime of Sc/Leu-MSs. The enhancement mechanism has been proven to be due to recrystallization-induced structural confinement and aluminized reconstruction. This strategy is proposed to improve the phos. efficiency of Sc/Leu-MSs with the following benefits: (i) Increase in lifetime. Al2(SO4)3 treatment can result in a maximum lifetime increase of 4.42 times. (ii) Increase in phos. intensity. Al2(SO4)3 treatment can cause a maximum phos. intensity increase of 24.08 times. (iii) Gain in mass. When compared to the initial mass of Sc/Leu-MSs, inorganic salt treatment can achieve a maximum mass increase of 27.16 times. (iv) Because this recrystallization strategy is not limited by the size of phosphorescent material, structural confinement of large-sized phosphorescent materials is possible. (v) By structural reconstruction, this recrystallization strategy may be used to adjust the phos. properties of Sc/Leu-MSs. Therefore, the proposed strategy can simultaneously improve phos. lifetime, quantum efficiency, and stability of phosphorescent materials. Finally, due to their unique optical properties, Sc/Leu-MSs have shown great application prospects in a variety of fields such as phosphorescent anti-counterfeiting, phosphorescent ink, and DUV-LEDs.

The authors appreciate the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21974046, 22176058, and 21977031) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (222201717003).

Li Ya Liang: Visualization, Validation, Investigation. Bin Bin Chen: Visualization, Investigation, Conceptualization, Supervision, Project administration. Yue Wang: Investigation, Validation. Ya Ting Gao: Investigation. Shuai Chang: Investigation. Meng Li Liu: Investigation. Da Wei Li: Conceptualization, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank Research Center of Analysis and Test of East China University of Science and Technology for the help on the characterization.
